title: Password Fortress
description: Learn the fundamentals of creating strong, unique passwords and why they matter for your digital security.

lessons.title: Why Passwords Matter
lessons.content:
Passwords are the first line of defense for your digital identity. A weak password is like leaving your front door unlocked — anyone can walk in.

**Key Facts:**
- 81% of data breaches involve weak or stolen passwords
- The average person has 100+ online accounts
- A 6-character password can be cracked in seconds

**What makes a strong password?**
- At least 12 characters long
- Mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
- No personal information (birthdays, names, etc.)
- Unique for every account

lessons.title: Password Managers: Your Digital Vault
lessons.content:
Remembering unique passwords for every account is impossible — that's where password managers come in.

**How they work:**
1. You create one strong master password
2. The manager generates and stores unique passwords for each site
3. Auto-fills login forms securely
4. Encrypts everything with military-grade encryption

**Popular options:** Bitwarden (free), 1Password, LastPass

**Pro tip:** Enable the password generator feature and set it to create 16+ character passwords with all character types.

knowledgeCheck.question: What is the minimum recommended length for a strong password?
What percentage of data breaches involve weak or stolen passwords?

title: Two-Factor Shield
description: Master two-factor authentication (2FA) and add an extra layer of security to all your important accounts.

lessons.title: Understanding 2FA
lessons.content:
Two-factor authentication requires two different types of verification before granting access.

**The three factors:**
1. **Something you know** — password, PIN
2. **Something you have** — phone, security key
3. **Something you are** — fingerprint, face scan

2FA combines two of these, so even if your password is stolen, attackers still can't access your account.

**Types of 2FA (best to worst):**
- Hardware security keys (YubiKey)
- Authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy)
- SMS codes (better than nothing, but vulnerable to SIM swapping)

lessons.title: Setting Up 2FA Everywhere
lessons.content:
**Priority accounts for 2FA:**
1. Email (it's the key to all other accounts)
2. Banking and financial accounts
3. Social media
4. Cloud storage
5. School/work accounts

**Setup steps:**
1. Go to account security settings
2. Find 'Two-factor authentication' or 'Two-step verification'
3. Choose your method (authenticator app recommended)
4. Scan the QR code with your authenticator app
5. Save backup codes in a secure location

**Important:** Always save your backup codes — if you lose your phone, these are your only way back in.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which 2FA method is considered the most secure?
Which account should you prioritize for 2FA setup?

title: Phishing Detective
description: Learn to identify phishing attempts across email, SMS, and social media before they trick you.

lessons.title: Anatomy of a Phishing Attack
lessons.content:
Phishing is a social engineering attack that tricks you into revealing sensitive information.

**Common phishing tactics:**
- **Urgency:** 'Your account will be closed in 24 hours!'
- **Authority:** Impersonating banks, government, or tech companies
- **Fear:** 'Suspicious activity detected on your account'
- **Reward:** 'You've won a free iPhone!'

**Red flags to watch for:**
- Misspelled domain names (amaz0n.com, paypa1.com)
- Generic greetings ('Dear Customer' instead of your name)
- Suspicious attachments
- Links that don't match the claimed sender
- Poor grammar and formatting

lessons.title: Real vs. Fake: Practical Analysis
lessons.content:
**How to verify a suspicious message:**
1. **Check the sender's actual email address** — hover over the name to see the real address
2. **Hover over links** (don't click!) — check if the URL matches the claimed website
3. **Look for HTTPS** — legitimate sites use secure connections
4. **Contact the company directly** — use their official website, not the link in the email

**If you've been phished:**
1. Change your password immediately
2. Enable 2FA
3. Monitor your accounts for suspicious activity
4. Report the phishing attempt

knowledgeCheck.question: Which is a common red flag of a phishing email?
What should you do if you suspect a phishing email from your bank?
options: Go directly to your bank's official website

title: Social Engineering Defense
description: Advanced techniques for defending against sophisticated social engineering attacks beyond basic phishing.

lessons.title: Beyond Email: Modern Attack Vectors
lessons.content:
Attackers have evolved beyond simple email phishing.

**Modern social engineering tactics:**
- **Smishing:** Phishing via SMS ('Your package delivery failed, click here')
- **Vishing:** Voice phishing — fake tech support or bank calls
- **Spear phishing:** Targeted attacks using your personal info from social media
- **Pretexting:** Creating a fake scenario to extract information
- **Baiting:** Leaving infected USB drives in public places

**Why it works:**
Social engineering exploits human psychology — trust, fear, curiosity, and helpfulness. Technical defenses can't fully protect against human manipulation.

lessons.title: Building Your Defense Mindset
lessons.content:
**The STOP method:**
1. **S**top — Pause before reacting to urgent requests
2. **T**hink — Does this request make sense? Would this company contact me this way?
3. **O**bserve — Check for red flags, verify through official channels
4. **P**rotect — Report suspicious activity, share awareness with others

**Advanced protection:**
- Use unique email addresses for different services
- Limit personal information shared on social media
- Be skeptical of unsolicited contact, even from known organizations
- Verify requests through a separate communication channel
- Keep software updated to patch known vulnerabilities

knowledgeCheck.question: What does the 'S' in the STOP method stand for?

title: Privacy Foundations
description: Understand your digital footprint and learn essential practices for protecting your personal data online.

lessons.title: Your Digital Footprint
lessons.content:
Every online action leaves a trace — your digital footprint.

**Active footprint** (what you intentionally share):
- Social media posts and profiles
- Online purchases and registrations
- Comments and reviews

**Passive footprint** (collected without your direct action):
- Browsing history and cookies
- Location data from your phone
- App usage patterns
- IP addresses

**Why it matters:**
- Employers and universities check digital footprints
- Data brokers collect and sell your information
- Cybercriminals use personal data for targeted attacks
- Once online, information is nearly impossible to fully remove

lessons.title: Essential Privacy Practices
lessons.content:
**Immediate actions to take:**
1. **Review privacy settings** on all social media platforms
2. **Use private browsing** or a privacy-focused browser
3. **Disable location sharing** unless actively needed
4. **Review app permissions** — remove access for apps that don't need it
5. **Use different email addresses** for different purposes

**Data minimization principle:**
Only share the minimum information needed for a service to function. Ask yourself: 'Do they really need this information?'

**Privacy tools:**
- Browser: Firefox with privacy extensions
- Search: DuckDuckGo
- Messaging: Signal or WhatsApp (end-to-end encrypted)
- VPN for public networks

knowledgeCheck.question: What is a 'passive' digital footprint?
options: Posts you make on social media
Data collected without your direct action

knowledgeCheck.question: What is the data minimization principle?
options: Sharing only the minimum information needed

title: Advanced Data Protection
description: Advanced strategies for data protection including encryption, secure communication, and privacy regulations.

lessons.title: Encryption and Secure Communication
lessons.content:
Encryption converts your data into unreadable code that only authorized parties can decrypt.

**Types of encryption:**
- **In transit:** Protects data as it travels (HTTPS, VPN)
- **At rest:** Protects stored data (device encryption, encrypted cloud storage)
- **End-to-end (E2E):** Only sender and recipient can read messages

**Practical encryption:**
- Enable full-disk encryption on your devices
- Use messaging apps with E2E encryption
- Check for HTTPS (padlock icon) on websites
- Use encrypted cloud storage for sensitive files
- Encrypt sensitive email attachments

lessons.title: Know Your Rights: UAE Data Protection
lessons.content:
The UAE has comprehensive data protection frameworks:

**Key principles:**
- **Consent:** Organizations must obtain your consent before collecting data
- **Purpose limitation:** Data should only be used for stated purposes
- **Data minimization:** Only necessary data should be collected
- **Right to access:** You can request to see what data organizations hold about you
- **Right to deletion:** You can request removal of your personal data

**What you can do:**
- Read privacy policies before agreeing
- Exercise your right to access your data
- Request deletion of unnecessary data
- Report data protection violations to relevant authorities

knowledgeCheck.question: What does end-to-end encryption mean?
options: Data is encrypted on the server
Only sender and recipient can read messages

knowledgeCheck.question: Under UAE data protection principles, what right allows you to see what data organizations have about you?

title: Device Lockdown
description: Essential steps to secure your phone, laptop, and other devices against common threats.

lessons.title: Securing Your Devices
lessons.content:
Your devices are treasure troves of personal information. Securing them is essential.

**Phone security essentials:**
- Use biometric lock (fingerprint/face) PLUS a strong PIN
- Enable device encryption (on by default on modern phones)
- Turn on Find My Device / Find My iPhone
- Enable remote wipe capability
- Install apps only from official stores

**Laptop security essentials:**
- Use a strong login password
- Enable full-disk encryption (BitLocker/FileVault)
- Set screen lock after 5 minutes of inactivity
- Keep your operating system updated
- Use built-in firewall

lessons.title: Software Updates and Malware Protection
lessons.content:
**Why updates matter:**
Software updates patch security vulnerabilities. Delaying updates leaves you exposed to known attacks.

**Update strategy:**
1. Enable automatic updates on all devices
2. Restart devices promptly when updates require it
3. Update apps regularly through official stores
4. Update your browser — it's a primary attack vector

**Malware protection:**
- Windows: Built-in Windows Defender is sufficient for most users
- Mac: Keep Gatekeeper and XProtect enabled
- Phone: Avoid sideloading apps
- All devices: Don't click suspicious links or download unknown attachments

knowledgeCheck.question: Why are software updates important for security?
What should you enable to protect a lost device?

title: Network Defense
description: Protect yourself on public and home networks with VPNs, firewall basics, and Wi-Fi security.

lessons.title: Wi-Fi Security Threats
lessons.content:
Public Wi-Fi networks are hunting grounds for cybercriminals.

**Common attacks on public Wi-Fi:**
- **Evil twin:** Fake Wi-Fi networks that mimic legitimate ones
- **Man-in-the-middle:** Intercepting data between your device and the network
- **Packet sniffing:** Capturing unencrypted data sent over the network
- **Session hijacking:** Stealing your active login sessions

**Safe practices:**
- Never access banking or sensitive accounts on public Wi-Fi
- Verify the network name with the establishment
- Use a VPN to encrypt all your traffic
- Forget public networks after use
- Turn off auto-connect for Wi-Fi

lessons.title: VPNs and Home Network Security
lessons.content:
**VPN (Virtual Private Network):**
A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel for all your internet traffic, protecting it from interception.

**When to use a VPN:**
- Always on public Wi-Fi
- When accessing sensitive information remotely
- When you want to prevent ISP tracking

**Home network security:**
1. Change your router's default admin password
2. Use WPA3 encryption (or WPA2 at minimum)
3. Create a guest network for visitors
4. Update router firmware regularly
5. Disable WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup)
6. Consider hiding your network SSID

**Router check:** Log into your router (usually 192.168.1.1) and review all security settings.

knowledgeCheck.question: What is an 'evil twin' attack?
options: A fake Wi-Fi network mimicking a real one

knowledgeCheck.question: What encryption standard should your home Wi-Fi use at minimum?
